title: Structural Contradictions in Military Autocracy
content:
Totalitarianism traditionally features three salient characteristics: a grand project to remake society and human nature, the total politicization of private life, and a reliance on rule by terror without proper legal procedures. While modern Chinese governance under Xi Jinping resembles this in some respects, it overall lacks the sustained mass mobilization seen in earlier eras. A more accurate description of the current system is an intensified autocracy that leverages technological and organizational reinforcement to tighten social control and bureaucracy. However, within the People's Liberation Army (PLA), a much stricter arrangement of power has been implemented. This system comes remarkably close to totalitarianism by elevating the chairman's absolute control over all military matters into the highest political principle.

Despite its elegant design, implementing absolute leadership within the military inevitably runs into a structural contradiction. The chairmanship system formally requires all command and management to be unified under one individual, yet the chairman lacks the time, bandwidth, and specialized expertise to oversee daily operations. Consequently, the daily operation of the military is delegated to trusted deputies, primarily the vice chairmen of the Central Military Commission. These proxies are expected to be mere executors of the chairman’s orders, but their professional expertise often grants them significant interpretative authority. By selectively implementing directives or quietly steering them in a more practical direction, these deputies gradually acquire their own form of power.

As these proxies become more powerful, they inevitably develop their own personnel networks within the military ranks. Once a personnel network takes shape, a secondary power center forms around the vice chairman, which the paramount leader instinctively perceives as a direct threat. Military power ceases to flow directly from the chairman to the troops and instead passes through professional gatekeepers who may filter facts and command loyalty. An absolute leadership system cannot tolerate this dispersion of authority. This fundamental friction is precisely why widespread anti-corruption campaigns have continued to aggressively target the highest levels of the military.
